title: Chapter 32: The Son
content: The tower room smells like leather and dust and something underneath both — something chemical, like old photographs left in the sun. I push the door open and the hinges don't creak. That surprises me. Everything at Bly creaks. But Peter's door opens smoothly, oiled into silence, as if even the architecture learned not to announce itself in his presence.
I step inside.

The room is smaller than I expected. I've been building it in my imagination for weeks — a cathedral, a laboratory, a war room. But it's just a room. A desk facing the lake. A chair behind it. Bookshelves on two walls, half-empty. A narrow bed in the corner, neatly made, though no one's slept in it in over a year. The ceiling slopes on both sides, closing in like hands pressing together.
My father lived here.

The thought still doesn't land properly. It keeps sliding off the surface of my mind like water on glass. My father lived here and designed a machine that ran on women's lives and I am standing in it, breathing it, touching the desk where his hands rested while he worked out the rules.

I circle the desk slowly. The wood is dark walnut, scratched near the edges where rings from cups and glasses have worn through the polish. A drawer on the right side hangs open a quarter inch. I don't open it. Not yet.
The chair.

It's a leather armchair, high-backed, the color of dried blood. The leather is worn on both armrests — paler patches where hands gripped it thousands of times. The seat cushion has a permanent depression, the shape of a man who sat here for hours, days, years, watching the lake through the window and building his game.
I sit down.

The leather is cold through my clothes. The chair is too big for me, or I'm too small for it — the same thing, from different angles. I put my hands on the armrests and my fingers fall into the worn grooves. Peter's hands were larger than mine. Or maybe they weren't. Maybe Caroline's hands were small like mine and he overlapped her in every direction.
I face the lake.

The window is enormous — the whole upper curve of the tower opens onto the water. Late afternoon light spreads across the surface in broken sheets. The lake looks flat from up here, manageable, like something you could fold up and put in your pocket. I imagine him looking at this. Seeing the same water. Thinking: I can control this. I can control all of it. All I need is a system and players and rules.
What did he feel? Satisfaction? The clean pleasure of an engineer watching his machine run?
Or was it just — nothing? Just the absence of anything that wasn't the Game?

I lean back in Peter's chair and the leather creaks. The sound is loud in the silence. The house is so quiet since Flora left. Since Mrs. Grose went to Sarah Chen's. The walls seem to hold their breath.
"You found it."
I don't jump. I should, but I don't. Maybe I've been waiting for this. Maybe I came here specifically so he would find me.

Miles stands in the doorway. Not inside — the threshold. One hand on the frame, as if he needs it to stay upright. He looks ruined. The word comes to me unbidden and it's the right one: ruined, like a building after a fire, the walls still standing but the interior gutted. His face is thinner than it was three days ago. The skin under his eyes has a bruised quality that isn't quite purple, isn't quite gray. His hair is unwashed. He's wearing the same shirt I saw him in yesterday — I can tell because there's a small stain near the second button, something brown, coffee or tea, and it's still there.

He doesn't smile. That's the worst part. Not because I want him to, but because I've realized I was waiting for the smile — the mask, the performance — and its absence makes the room feel wrong. Unfurnished.
"Can I come in?" he asks.
It's his room. His father's room. But he asks me, and something in the asking makes my throat tight.
"It's your house," I say.

He steps inside. Then he does something I don't expect: he doesn't come to the desk. He doesn't take the other chair — there is one, a straight-backed wooden chair in the corner, probably for visitors who didn't stay long. Instead he crosses to the wall opposite the window and sits on the floor. His back against the bookshelf. His knees drawn up. He rests his forearms on his knees and looks at me.
I'm in Peter's chair. He's on the floor.
"You sat in his chair," he says. Not an accusation. An observation.
"I wanted to see what he saw."
"And?"
I look at the lake again. The light is shifting toward gold, the way it does before sunset. "It's just a lake," I say. "I thought there would be more."
Miles makes a sound that might be a laugh if it had any air in it. "There's never more. That's the secret. There's never more than what you put there yourself."
I wait. The silence stretches between us like a held breath.
"I need to tell you something," he says. "And I need you to listen. Not respond. Not judge. Just listen. Can you do that?"
The request is so plain, so unadorned, that I nod before I can think about whether it's a manipulation.
Miles looks at his hands. The knuckles are raw — he's been hitting something. Walls, probably. I've done that. I know what it feels like when the wall wins.

"He trained us when we were seven," Miles says. "Peter. He sat us down in the drawing room and told us we were going to learn a game. A special game. Just for us. And we believed him because we were seven and he was our—" He stops. Swallows. "He was our father. Or the closest thing we had to one. Uncle Peter, who lived in the tower and let us visit and never raised his voice."
I don't speak. He asked me to listen.

"The Game was never about the governesses. Not for him. They were — raw material. Variables. He needed them to make the system run, but the system wasn't for them." Miles's voice is steady in a way that makes me think he's been rehearsing this for years, maybe his whole life, and only now has an audience. "The Game was about control. Peter controlled this house by controlling the Game. Everything — the staff schedules, the guest rotations, the maintenance cycles, the meal plans — it all ran through the Game. He built a machine that governed a household and we were the engine."
His fingers dig into his forearms. I can see the pressure whitening his skin.

"When he died, the Game was the only structure we had. Flora and I. We were twelve. No parents. No family but each other. No school — we'd been tutored here our whole lives. No friends. Just this house and the Game and each other." He looks up at me. "Without the Game, we were just two orphans in a big house with nothing to do. No identity. No purpose. No meaning. The Game gave us all of that."
I want to say: You were children. You were twelve years old. But he asked me not to respond, and I hold the words in my mouth like stones.
"It's not an excuse," Miles says. His voice cracks on the second word. "I know that. I know it doesn't explain what we did to Rebecca. To the others. It doesn't undo any of it. But it's the truth."

He holds my gaze. His eyes are dry. Not because he's hiding — because he's past tears. There's a desiccated quality to him, like something left in the sun too long. All the moisture has evaporated and what's left is just structure. Bone and tendon and the architecture of someone who was once a person.
"I'm done listening," I say.
He flinches. I watch him prepare for the blow — whatever he thinks I'm about to say.
"You could have been my sister."
The words hang in the air between us. They don't make sense. I wait for them to resolve into something I understand and they don't.
"What?" I say.

"If Peter had married your mother." Miles speaks carefully, as if he's handling something made of glass. "If things had been different. If Caroline had come to Bly instead of leaving. We'd have grown up together. You and me and Flora. A family. Instead of—" He gestures at the room, the house, the whole sprawling catastrophe. "This."
The alternative timeline hits me like a wall.

Not like a wall. Like a freeway. Six lanes of it, all moving, all carrying the same impossible traffic. I could have been raised here. At Bly. I could have been Peter's daughter in name and not just in blood. I could have grown up in these hallways, learning the Game the way Flora learned it — as first language, as mother tongue. I could have been a player instead of a governess. I could have been the one standing on the shore with a camera, laughing while a woman drowned.
I could have been Miles.

That's the horror. Not that monsters exist — I've known that since I was nine years old and my mother stopped being my mother. The horror is that I was one permutation away from being one. One decision. One marriage certificate. One move in a game I never asked to play, and instead of sitting in this chair, I'd be sitting on that floor, confessing to someone who got lucky.
Or I'd be worse. I'd be someone who didn't confess at all.
The leather of Peter's chair creaks as I grip the armrests. My knuckles are white. I'm his daughter. I'm sitting in his chair. My hands fit the grooves his hands made.
"It wouldn't have been different," I say, and I don't know if it's a lie. "I wouldn't have — I'm not like you."

"You're exactly like me." Miles's voice is quiet. Not cruel. Not gentle. Just true, the way things are true when someone has stopped caring about the effect of the truth. "That's what frightens you. You walked into this house a stranger and within weeks you were running the Game better than Flora ever did. You didn't learn it. You remembered it. Same way I did."
"Peter didn't train me."
"Peter didn't have to. It's in the blood."

I want to argue. I want to stand up from this chair and walk out of this room and never come back. But my legs won't move and my mouth won't form the words because a part of me — a part I keep locked in a room with no windows — knows he's right. The Game made sense to me the way algebra makes sense to some people: not because I studied it, but because my brain is built for it. Pattern recognition. System optimization. The ability to see human behavior as a set of inputs and outputs.
Peter's brain was built for it too.
And Peter was my father.

The lake is turning red. Sunset bleeding across the surface. I watch the color spread and think about Caroline standing on this same shore, looking up at this same tower, making the decision that saved me. She left. She took me away. She chose poverty and shame and a life of trying to forget over raising her daughter inside Peter's machine.
And it destroyed her. But it saved me.
I'm only here because she left.
"Help me end it."

Miles's voice breaks on the last word. Actually breaks, splinters, fractures into something unrecognizable. I look at him and his face is crumpling, not into tears but into something worse — the shape of a person who has nothing left to hold himself together with.

"Help me burn the Game down," he says. "I don't know how. I've never known anything else. My whole life — Flora's whole life — it's been the Game. It's the only thing we know how to do. And I—" He presses the heels of his hands against his eyes. "I watched a woman die and I laughed. I remember laughing. I remember the sound of it. And I can't — I can't make it stop. It plays on a loop in my head. Every day. Every night. That laugh. My laugh. And I don't know how to be someone who didn't laugh."
He drops his hands. His eyes are red-rimmed but still dry. Hollow.

"But you," he says. "You're Peter's daughter and Caroline's daughter. You lived outside the machine. You know what the rest of the world looks like. You're the only one who could build something in its place."

The room is dark now. The sunset has drained away and the lake is just black glass reflecting nothing. I can barely see Miles on the floor — just the shape of him, knees drawn up, back against the books.
I sit in Peter's chair.
He sits on the cold floor.

Two children of the same monstrous father. One raised inside the machine, one raised outside it. And the distance between us isn't the width of this room — it's a single decision Caroline made twenty-three years ago. That's all. One decision.

He's asking me to trust him. That's what this is. Under the confession, under the truth, under the cracked voice and the raw knuckles and the filthy shirt — he's asking me to believe that he can change. That the person who laughed while Rebecca Jessel walked into the water isn't the person sitting on this floor now.

But here's what I know: vulnerability is not the same as trustworthiness. A man can weep and still lie. A man can confess and still manipulate. The most dangerous people aren't the ones who hide their damage — they're the ones who display it. Because damage, real damage, the kind you can't fake, makes people trust you. And trust is the most powerful weapon in any game.
I know this because Peter knew this. And Peter's brain is my brain.
I look at the lake. I look at Miles.
He waited for me to speak. He's still waiting. Patient in a way I've never seen him patient before — the stillness of someone who has already accepted that the answer might be no.

Could I build something? Take the skeleton of the Game — the structure, the architecture, the way it organizes human behavior — and repurpose it? Turn a weapon into a tool? A machine that eats people into a system that protects them?
I don't know.
But I know Peter never tried. Peter never once looked at his creation and asked whether it could be different. He only ever asked whether it could be more.

I could say yes. I could say no. I could walk out of this room and let the courts handle it and never think about Miles Ashford again. I could go back to London and find a flat and a job and a life, and I could leave Bly to whatever comes after — the legal proceedings, the investigations, the long slow demolition of a hundred years of secrets.
Or I could do something Peter never did.
I could change the game into something that doesn't hurt people.

But to do that, I'd have to trust Miles. I'd have to work beside him. Day after day. In this house, in these rooms, with the ghost of our shared father watching from the walls. I'd have to believe that the boy who was trained at seven and orphaned at twelve and laughed at twenty-one is capable of becoming someone else.
Miles watches me from the floor. Waiting. The last light from the lake catches his eyes and for a moment they look almost alive.
Can you build with someone who destroyed?
I don't know. I don't know. I don't know.
The tower room holds the question like a cup holds water. The house is silent. The lake is black. Peter's chair creaks beneath me, and I sit in it, and I do not answer yet.
